1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

94	/-! ## §1. The identity component of a group scheme — abstract substrate
95	
96	The identity component is an abstract feature of `k`-group schemes locally of
97	finite type. We package the definition + open-and-closed-subgroup-scheme
98	structure in the `GroupScheme` namespace so they are reusable outside the
99	Picard context.
100	
101	In this project, a "`k`-group scheme" is encoded as an
102	`Over (Spec (.of k))`-object `G` carrying a `[GrpObj G]` instance (the
103	group-object structure in the over-category). The locally-of-finite-type
104	hypothesis is the `[LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]` instance on the structural
105	morphism to the base.
106	
107	Blueprint references: `def:identity_component_group_scheme` +
108	`thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`). -/
109	
110	namespace GroupScheme
111	
112	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, the set of
113	connected components is finite.
114	
115	Proof: the map `irreducibleComponents α → ConnectedComponents α` sending each
116	irreducible component `C` to the connected component of an arbitrary chosen
117	point of `C` is well-defined (because `C` is preconnected, hence contained in
118	a single connected component) and surjective (because every point lies in
119	some irreducible component, namely `irreducibleComponent x`, which is a
120	subset of `connectedComponent x`). The source is finite by
121	`TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents`, so the
122	target is finite by `Finite.of_surjective`. -/
123	private lemma noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
124	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α] :
125	    Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := by
126	  classical
127	  have hfin : (irreducibleComponents α).Finite :=
128	    TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents
129	  haveI : Finite ↥(irreducibleComponents α) := hfin.to_subtype
130	  refine Finite.of_surjective
131	    (fun C : ↥(irreducibleComponents α) =>
132	      (ConnectedComponents.mk : α → ConnectedComponents α)
133	        ((C.property : Maximal IsIrreducible C.val).prop.nonempty.some)) ?_
134	  intro c
135	  obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := ConnectedComponents.surjective_coe c
136	  refine ⟨⟨irreducibleComponent x, irreducibleComponent_mem_irreducibleComponents x⟩, ?_⟩
137	  rw [ConnectedComponents.coe_eq_coe]
138	  refine (connectedComponent_eq ?_).symm
139	  exact irreducibleComponent_subset_connectedComponent
140	    (isIrreducible_irreducibleComponent (x := x)).nonempty.some_mem
141	
142	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, each
143	connected component is open.
144	
145	Proof: `ConnectedComponents α` is totally disconnected
146	(`ConnectedComponents.totallyDisconnectedSpace`), hence T1
147	(`TotallyDisconnectedSpace.t1Space`), and finite by
148	`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`. A finite T1 space is discrete
149	(`Finite.instDiscreteTopology`), so the singleton
150	`{ConnectedComponents.mk x}` is open in the quotient. The preimage of this
151	singleton under the continuous quotient map `ConnectedComponents.mk` is
152	`connectedComponent x` (`connectedComponents_preimage_singleton`), which is
153	therefore open. -/
154	private lemma noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent
155	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α]
156	    (x : α) :
157	    IsOpen (connectedComponent x) := by
158	  haveI : Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
159	  haveI : DiscreteTopology (ConnectedComponents α) := inferInstance
160	  have h := (isOpen_discrete
161	    ({(ConnectedComponents.mk x : ConnectedComponents α)} : Set _)).preimage
162	    ConnectedComponents.continuous_coe
163	  rwa [connectedComponents_preimage_singleton] at h
164	
165	/-- Helper (iter-188): the **`LocallyConnectedSpace` instance** for the
166	underlying topological space of a `k`-scheme `G.left` whose structural
167	morphism `G.hom : G.left ⟶ Spec k` is locally of finite type.
168	
169	The substantive content is EGA I 6.1.9: a locally Noetherian topological
170	space has open connected components — equivalently, is locally connected.
171	Pushed through the chain: `Spec k` is Noetherian (a field has Noetherian
172	spectrum); `LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom + IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k) ⟹
173	IsLocallyNoetherian G.left` (Mathlib's
174	`AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian`); and the
175	implication `IsLocallyNoetherian X ⟹ LocallyConnectedSpace X.toTopCat`
176	is the iter-188 project-side helper (`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`
177	+ `noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent`).
178	
179	The classical proof: each point `y ∈ G.left` has an open affine
180	neighbourhood `W = Spec R` with `R` Noetherian, hence `|W|` is a Noetherian
181	topological space; in a Noetherian space, each `connectedComponent y` is
182	clopen (finite irreducible components ⟹ finite connected components ⟹
183	finite T1 quotient ⟹ discrete quotient ⟹ singletons clopen in quotient ⟹
184	preimages clopen). Pulled back along the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left`,
185	this gives an open connected neighbourhood of `y` inside any open `F`
186	containing `y`. -/
187	private instance identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace
188	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
189	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
190	    [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
191	    LocallyConnectedSpace G.left := by
192	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian G.left := LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian G.hom
193	  rw [locallyConnectedSpace_iff_subsets_isOpen_isConnected]
194	  intro x F hF
195	  -- Extract an open neighbourhood `T ⊆ F` of `x`.
196	  obtain ⟨T, hTF, hT, hxT⟩ := mem_nhds_iff.mp hF
197	  -- Find an affine open `W ⊆ T` with `x ∈ W`.
198	  obtain ⟨W, hW, hxW, hWT⟩ :=
199	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := G.left) (U := ⟨T, hT⟩) hxT
200	  haveI : IsNoetherianRing Γ(G.left, W) :=
201	    IsLocallyNoetherian.component_noetherian ⟨W, hW⟩
202	  haveI hNoethW : TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace ↥W :=
203	    noetherianSpace_of_isAffineOpen W hW
204	  -- Inside `W` (Noetherian) the connected component of `x` is open; its image
205	  -- under the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left` is the connected open neighbourhood
206	  -- we want.
207	  let xW : ↥W := ⟨x, hxW⟩
208	  refine ⟨(Subtype.val : ↥W → G.left) '' connectedComponent xW, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
209	  · rintro _ ⟨z, _, rfl⟩
210	    exact hTF (hWT z.2)
211	  · exact W.isOpen.isOpenMap_subtype_val _
212	      (noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent xW)
213	  · exact ⟨xW, mem_connectedComponent, rfl⟩
214	  · exact isConnected_connectedComponent.image _ continuous_subtype_val.continuousOn
215	
216	/-- The image of the identity section `e : Spec k → G` (well-defined as a
217	single point of `|G|` because `Spec k` is a topological singleton). -/
218	private noncomputable def identitySectionPoint
219	    {k : Type u} [Field k] (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] : G.left :=
220	  ((MonObj.one (X := G)).left.base :
221	      ↥(Spec (.of k)) → G.left) (default : Spec (.of k))
222	
223	/-- Helper (iter-186; iter-187 closed): the **clopen carrier `Set`** of
224	the identity component of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type,
225	packaged as a `G.left.Opens`. The carrier set is `connectedComponent x`
226	for `x = e(*)` the image of the identity section
227	(`identitySectionPoint G`); openness is `isOpen_connectedComponent` which
228	needs the `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance above
229	(EGA I 6.1.9). -/
230	private noncomputable def identityComponentCarrier {k : Type u} [Field k]
231	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
232	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
233	    G.left.Opens :=
234	  ⟨connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G), isOpen_connectedComponent⟩
235	
236	/-- The **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
237	finite type.
238	
239	Encoded as a `k`-scheme: an object of `Over (Spec (.of k))`, carrying the
240	intended substantive identity "this is the connected component of `|G|`
241	containing the identity section `e`, equipped with the open-subscheme
242	structure inherited from `G`". The associated open immersion
243	`IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` is the content of
244	`IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`.
245	
246	iter-186 body: built from the `identityComponentCarrier G` helper — the
247	open subscheme of `G` whose underlying topological space is the connected
248	component of `|G|` through the image of the identity section. The
249	structure morphism is the inherited `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom`.
250	The substantive content (the actual carrier `Set`, plus its openness from
251	EGA I 6.1.9: locally Noetherian spaces have open connected components)
252	lives in the typed-sorry body of `identityComponentCarrier`. -/
253	noncomputable def IdentityComponent {k : Type u} [Field k]
254	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
255	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
256	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
257	  Over.mk ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)
258	
259	/-- **The identity component is an open and closed subgroup scheme.**
260	
261	The bundled statement of Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3): the identity
262	component `G^0 = IdentityComponent G` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
263	finite type comes with a morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` (in
264	`Over (Spec (.of k))`) whose underlying scheme morphism is both an open
265	immersion and a closed immersion (i.e. the inclusion of a clopen
266	subscheme).
267	
268	The full Kleiman conclusion also packages the group-subscheme property (the
269	inclusion is a homomorphism of `k`-group schemes), finite-type-ness over `k`
270	(`LocallyOfFiniteType` + quasi-compactness), geometric irreducibility, and
271	base-change-commutation. Those refinements live as separate instances /
272	follow-up lemmas in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the clopen
273	open-immersion conclusion as a Nonempty-witness.
274	
275	iter-186 body: the inclusion morphism is `Over.homMk (identityComponentCarrier G).ι`,
276	with the over-category compatibility holding by definition of
277	`IdentityComponent G`. The `.left` of this morphism is
278	`(identityComponentCarrier G).ι`, an open immersion by the global
279	`Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι` instance. For the closed-immersion
280	half we apply `IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion` to the open immersion
281	and reduce to `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G) : Set _)`. The
282	remaining inline `sorry` is the substantive content: the carrier of
283	`identityComponentCarrier G` is closed, by "closure of a connected subspace
284	is connected" applied to the connected component through the image of the
285	identity section. -/
286	theorem IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
287	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
288	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
289	    Nonempty {f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G //
290	        IsOpenImmersion f.left ∧ IsClosedImmersion f.left} := by
291	  -- Over-category compatibility for the underlying open immersion.
292	  have hcomp : (identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom = (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
293	    simp [IdentityComponent]
294	  -- The over-morphism witness.
295	  let f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G :=
296	    Over.homMk (U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G) (identityComponentCarrier G).ι hcomp
297	  refine ⟨⟨f, ?_, ?_⟩⟩
298	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` (definitionally; `Over.homMk_left` simp).
299	    -- An open immersion by `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι`.
300	    change IsOpenImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
301	    infer_instance
302	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` is a preimmersion (open immersion ⟹
303	    -- immersion ⟹ preimmersion). Its range is the carrier set (`Scheme.Opens.range_ι`),
304	    -- and closure of that connected subset is itself (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3):
305	    -- the connected component through the identity is closed in `|G|`).
306	    change IsClosedImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
307	    apply IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion
308	    rw [Scheme.Opens.range_ι]
309	    -- The carrier set is `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`, which is
310	    -- closed by `isClopen_connectedComponent.1` (with the
311	    -- `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance providing the closedness
312	    -- half of EGA I 6.1.9: connected components of a locally Noetherian topological
313	    -- space are clopen).
314	    change IsClosed (connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G))
315	    exact isClopen_connectedComponent.1
316	
317	/-- **The identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism.**
318	
319	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (b): the clopen subscheme `G^0`
320	inherits a `k`-group-scheme structure from `G`, and the inclusion morphism
321	`G^0 ↪ G` (from `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`) is compatible
322	with the group laws on source and target. The statement-level pin asserts
323	the existence of the inherited `GrpObj` structure; the compatibility of
324	the inclusion with the group operations is the substantive content of
325	Kleiman's argument (the product `G^0 ×_k G^0` is connected by
326	EGA IV₂ 4.5.8; the group-multiplication map sends this connected subset
327	containing the identity into the connected component `G^0`).
328	
329	The full statement (existence of `GrpObj (IdentityComponent G)` *together*
330	with the homomorphism-compatibility of the inclusion in the relevant
331	`Hom`-form) lives in iter-188+. -/
332	theorem IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism {k : Type u} [Field k]
333	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
334	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
335	    Nonempty (GrpObj (IdentityComponent G)) :=
336	  sorry
337	
338	/-- **The identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible.**
339	
340	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (c): the open subgroup `G^0` of
341	a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type is itself
342	locally-of-finite-type-plus-quasi-compact (i.e., of finite type) over `k`,
343	and is geometrically irreducible. The proof reduces (after base change to
344	`\bar k`) to picking a nonempty smooth affine open subset `U ⊆ G^0`; the
345	translates `hg⁻¹U` give an open cover of `G^0` by smooth, hence irreducible,
346	neighbourhoods, so `G^0` is locally irreducible at every closed point; with
347	connectedness this gives irreducibility globally (EGA I 6.1.10). The image
348	`α(U × U) = G^0` is the image of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme
349	`U × U`, so `G^0` is quasi-compact. -/
350	theorem IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible
351	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
352	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
353	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
354	    LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom ∧
355	      QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom ∧
356	      GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom :=
357	  sorry
358	
359	/-- **Formation of the identity component commutes with base change.**
360	
361	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (d): for any field extension
362	`K/k`, the natural comparison map `(G^0)_K → (G_K)^0` is an isomorphism of
363	`K`-schemes. The proof: `G^0` is geometrically connected as a connected
364	scheme with a `k`-rational point (EGA IV₂ 4.5.14), so its base change
365	`(G^0)_K` remains connected; it is also open and closed in `G_K`, so
366	coincides with the identity component `(G_K)^0`.
367	
368	The statement-level pin asserts existence of: a `K`-group-scheme structure
369	on the base change `G ×_{Spec k} Spec K` (with appropriate
370	locally-of-finite-type instance), and an isomorphism on underlying schemes
371	identifying the two iterated constructions. The iso witness is a typed
372	sorry pending iter-188+ implementation; the substantive `GrpObj` is
373	provided by `Scheme.GrpObjAsOverPullback` and is propagated through. -/
374	theorem IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso {k : Type u} [Field k]
375	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]
376	    (K : Type u) [Field K] [Algebra k K] :
377	    let φ : Spec (CommRingCat.of K) ⟶ Spec (CommRingCat.of k) :=
378	      Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap k K))
379	    let G_K : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of K)) :=
380	      Over.mk (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.snd G.hom φ)
381	    Nonempty (Σ' (_grpInst : GrpObj G_K)
382	                 (_locFTInst : LocallyOfFiniteType G_K.hom),
383	      (IdentityComponent G_K).left ≅
384	        CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback (IdentityComponent G).hom φ) :=
385	  sorry

387	end GroupScheme

389	/-! ## §2. The identity component of the Picard scheme
390	
391	We specialise the abstract identity-component substrate to
392	`G = PicScheme C`, the Picard scheme of a smooth proper geometrically
393	integral curve `C/k` (from sibling `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean`).
394	
395	Blueprint reference: `def:pic_zero_subscheme` (Kleiman §5 opening + Prp.
396	`prp:pic0`). -/
397	
398	namespace Scheme
399	
400	/-- The **identity component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.
401	
402	Defined as the identity component
403	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)` of the Picard scheme
404	`Pic_{C/k}` (from sibling `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean`,
405	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`). By
406	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`, `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an
407	open and closed subgroup scheme of `Pic_{C/k}` of finite type over `k`,
408	geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with extension of the
409	base field.
410	
411	iter-186+: the body unwinds to `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)`
412	once the `[LocallyOfFiniteType (PicScheme C).hom]` instance lands on
413	`PicScheme C` (it follows from the Kleiman §4 structure: `Pic_{C/k}` is
414	locally of finite type as the disjoint union of open quasi-projective
415	`k`-subschemes). For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
416	noncomputable def Pic0Scheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
417	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
418	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
419	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
420	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
421	  sorry

423	/-! ## §3. The degree map
424	
425	The disjoint-union structure of `Pic_{C/k}` (a disjoint union of open
426	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via
427	`PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient`) stratifies its `T`-points by the leading
428	coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
429	relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation. On `k`-points this gives the
430	**degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
431	
432	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree_pic` (Milne III.1, p.~88). -/
433	
434	namespace PicScheme
435	
436	/-- The **degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
437	
438	Sends a `k`-point `λ ∈ Pic_{C/k}(k)` --- a morphism
439	`Spec k ⟶ (PicScheme C).left` --- to the leading coefficient of the
440	Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf `L` on `C` (relative
441	to a fixed degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`). By Riemann--Roch,
442	`χ(C, L ⊗ O_C(n)) = n · deg L + 1 - g`, so the degree is the leading
443	coefficient of `Φ_L(n)`, well-defined on the isomorphism class `[L]` and on
444	the `k`-point `λ` (because `PicScheme C` represents the étale-sheafified
445	relative Picard functor).
446	
447	The degree map is a group homomorphism for the additive structure on
448	`Pic_{C/k}(k)` (tensor product on `L`) and the standard `(ℤ, +)`. The full
449	group-homomorphism refinement / functoriality in `k` lives as a follow-up
450	lemma in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the underlying function.
451	
452	iter-186+: the body extracts the representing invertible sheaf from
453	`(PicScheme.representable C)`, forms its Hilbert polynomial via the project's
454	Hilbert-polynomial machinery (sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`), and returns
455	the leading coefficient. For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed
456	`sorry`. -/
457	noncomputable def degree {k : Type u} [Field k]
458	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
459	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
460	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
461	    (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ :=
462	  sorry
463	
464	end PicScheme

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}

117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite

127	[Identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism]
128	  \label{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
129	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
130	
131	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
132	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
133	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
134	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
135	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
136	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
137	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}

139	  type. The clopen inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\)
140	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of
141	  \(k\)-group schemes: \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure
142	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over

149	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
150	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
152	
153	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
154	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
155	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
156	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
157	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
158	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
159	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}

161	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
162	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}

167	
168	\begin{theorem}
169	
170	\leanok
171	[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
172	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
174	
175	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
176	  % verbatim quote of (3) reproduced at
177	  % \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} above
178	  % (references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
179	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
180	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
181	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
182	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
183	  type, and \(K/k\) a field extension. The natural map
184	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\), induced by base-changing the inclusion of
185	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} along \(\Spec K \to \Spec k\)
186	  and using the universal property of the identity component on
187	  \(G_K\), is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.

189	
190	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
191	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
192	% (3).)
193	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
194	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
195	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
196	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
197	% \(G^0\) is open too.
198	%
199	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
200	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
201	% extending \(k\).
202	%
203	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
204	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
205	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
206	%
207	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
208	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
209	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
210	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
211	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
212	% [\dots]
213	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
214	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
215	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
216	% [\dots]
217	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
218	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
219	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
220	\begin{proof}
221	  We establish
222	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
223	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
224	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
225	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
226	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
227	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
228	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
229	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
230	
231	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
232	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
233	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
234	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
235	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
236	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
237	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
238	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
239	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
240	
241	  \emph{Base-change commutation
242	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
243	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
244	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
245	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
246	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
247	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
248	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
249	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
250	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
251	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
252	
253	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
254	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
255	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
256	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
257	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
258	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
259	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
260	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
261	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
262	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
263	
264	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
265	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
266	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
267	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
268	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
269	  \(\overline k\), the product
270	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
271	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
272	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
273	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
274	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
275	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
276	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
277	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
278	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
279	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
280	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
281	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
282	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
283	\end{proof}

285	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
286	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
287	
288	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
289	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
290	
291	\begin{definition}\leanok
292	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
293	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}

329	\section{The degree map}
330	\label{sec:pic_degree}
331	
332	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
333	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
334	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
335	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
336	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
337	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
338	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
339	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
340	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
341	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
342	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
343	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
344	degree.
345	
346	\begin{definition}\leanok
347	[Degree of a Picard class]
348	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
350	  
351	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
352	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
353	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
355	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
356	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
357	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
358	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
359	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
360	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
361	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
362	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
363	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
364	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
365	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
366	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
367	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
368	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
369	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
370	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
371	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
372	  \[
373	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
374	  \]
375	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
376	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
377	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
378	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
379	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
380	  \[
381	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
382	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
383	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
384	  \]
385	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
386	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
387	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
388	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
389	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
390	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
391	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
392	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
393	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
394	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
395	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
396	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
397	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
398	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
399	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
400	  of degree \(d\).
401	\end{definition}

403	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
404	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
405	
406	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
407	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
408	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
409	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
410	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
411	automatically.

520	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
521	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
522	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
523	% identity-component substrate.)
524	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
525	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
526	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
527	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
528	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
529	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
530	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
531	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
532	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
533	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
534	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
535	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
536	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
537	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
538	% everywhere."
539	\begin{proof}
540	  We establish
541	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
542	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
543	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
544	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
545	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
546	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
547	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
548	
549	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
550	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
551	  conjuncts from four inputs.
552	
553	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
554	  By
555	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
556	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
557	  type by
558	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
559	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
560	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
561	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
562	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
564	
565	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
566	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
567	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
568	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
569	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
570	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
571	
572	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
573	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
574	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
575	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
576	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
577	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
578	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
579	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
580	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
581	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
582	
583	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
584	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
585	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
586	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
587	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
588	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
589	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
590	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
591	
592	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
593	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
594	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
595	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
596	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
597	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
598	
599	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
600	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
601	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
602	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
603	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
604	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
605	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
606	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
607	  the open identity component, and
608	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
609	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
610	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
611	
612	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
613	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
614	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
615	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
616	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
617	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
618	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
619	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
620	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
621	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
622	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
623	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
624	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
625	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
626	  zero on \(C\)'').
627	\end{proof}

679	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
680	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
681	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
682	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
683	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
684	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
685	    component.
686	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
687	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
688	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
689	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
690	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
691	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
692	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
693	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
694	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
695	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
696	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
697	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
698	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
699	    underlying scheme).
700	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
701	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
702	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
703	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
704	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
705	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
706	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
707	\end{itemize}
708	
709	The abstract identity-component substrate
710	(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
711	conclusion-specific theorems
712	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},
713	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism},
714	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},
715	\texttt{IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}) is new project material: it
716	is not currently in Mathlib, and the Lean skeleton is owed in a
717	follow-up iteration. The downstream
718	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety},
719	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.finrank\_eq\_genus}, and
720	\texttt{Pic0Scheme.kPoints\_iff\_kerDegree} consume Kleiman~\S 5
721	Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac and Milne~\S III.1, both of which currently
722	live only at the blueprint level; their Lean specialisation to the
723	smooth-proper-curve case is the main substantive step of A.3.
724	
725	\section{Out of scope}
726	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
727	
728	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
729	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
730	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
731	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
732	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
733	
734	\begin{itemize}
735	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
736	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
737	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
738	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
739	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
740	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
741	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
742	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
743	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
744	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
745	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
746	    Route~A.
747	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
748	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
749	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
750	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
751	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
752	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
753	\end{itemize}
754

764	    Archon-internal abstract substrate; no cross-chapter

9	# FGA representability of the Picard scheme (A.2.c)
10	
11	This file is the **A.2.c** assembly file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
12	project's positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It wires the
13	Quot-scheme engine (A.2.b, sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`) and the
14	étale-sheafified relative Picard functor (A.1.c, sibling
15	`Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`) into the FGA representability statement for
16	`Pic_{C/k}`, following Kleiman, "The Picard scheme", §4 (cf. FGA Explained
17	Ch. 9 §9.4; arXiv:math/0504020).
18	
19	## Status (iter-176 Lane I file-skeleton)
20	
21	This file is the **iter-176 Lane I** file-skeleton (re-dispatch of the iter-175
22	Lane I that died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without ever
23	calling `Write`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned declarations carries the
24	*intended* substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
25	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex`) with a `sorry`
26	body. The bodies are iter-177+ work, gated on the sibling chapters
27	`Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean` (A.1.c) and `Picard/QuotScheme.lean` (A.2.b)
28	landing their pinned declarations.
29	
30	The 5 pinned declarations are:
31	
32	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **Picard scheme**
33	   `Pic_{C/k}` as an object of `Over (Spec k)`, the witness extracted from
34	   the `Functor.RepresentableBy` produced by `representable`.
35	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap` (def, ~6 LOC) — the
36	   **Abel map** `A_{C/k} : Div_{C/k} ⟶ Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}` as a natural
37	   transformation of presheaves on `(Sch/k)^op`.
38	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient` (theorem, ~15 LOC)
39	   — the Altman–Kleiman **smooth-proper quotient lemma** (Kleiman §4
40	   Lem. `lm:qt`) packaging the descent from a flat-and-proper equivalence
41	   relation `R ⊆ Z ×_k Z` on a quasi-projective `k`-scheme `Z` to a
42	   representing quotient `Q`.
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable` (theorem, ~12 LOC) —
44	   the **FGA representability theorem** (Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main`,
45	   specialised via Cor. `cor:algsch` to `S = Spec k` and `X = C` a smooth
46	   proper geometrically integral curve): the étale-sheafified relative
47	   Picard functor is represented by `PicScheme`.
48	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure` (instance,
49	   ~6 LOC) — the **group-scheme structure** on `PicScheme`: a `GrpObj`
50	   instance obtained by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure
51	   on `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`.
52	
53	## Note on type expressivity
54	
55	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
56	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
57	
58	- `PicScheme C : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a scheme over `k`, not a
59	  tautological `Spec k`.
60	- `abelMap C : divFunctor C ⟶ picSharp C` — a genuine natural
61	  transformation of presheaves, not `𝟙 _` (it sends a relative effective
62	  divisor to its associated invertible sheaf).
63	- `smoothProperQuotient` carries the four Kleiman §4 Lem. `lm:qt`
64	  hypotheses (Z represented, R representable, smooth+proper first
65	  projection, étale surjection) and concludes `P.IsRepresentable`. The
66	  hypotheses are not vacuous: any one of (i)–(iv) can fail for a
67	  non-quotient morphism of presheaves.
68	- `representable C : (picSharp C).RepresentableBy (PicScheme C)` — the
69	  substantive Yoneda-bijection structure between `Hom(T, PicScheme C)` and
70	  `picSharp C |_T`.
71	- `groupSchemeStructure C : GrpObj (PicScheme C)` — a genuine group-object
72	  structure, with multiplication / inverse / unit morphisms satisfying the
73	  group axioms.
74	
75	The two file-internal auxiliary defs `picSharp` and `divFunctor` (placeholders
76	for the sibling-chapter functors `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}` and `Div_{C/k}`) are
77	typed-`sorry` *carriers* used to express the 5 pinned declarations' types
78	without forward-referencing into Lean files that have not yet landed. They
79	ship in the `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme` namespace, marked with

82	(forward refs `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp` and
83	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.divFunctor` per their blueprint `\lean{...}`
84	pins). Their bodies collapse to a one-line `:= ...` re-export at that
85	point.

193	/-! ## §2. The Abel map — line-bundle / Quot correspondence
194	
195	The bridge from line bundles on `C ×_k T` to the Quot scheme is the **Abel
196	map**: a relative effective divisor `D ⊆ C ×_k T` over `T` (an `S`-flat
197	closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a `T`-point of the
198	Hilbert functor `Hilb_{C/k}(T)` (hence of `Div_{C/k}(T)`), and the dual
199	ideal sheaf `O_{C ×_k T}(D) := I_D⁻¹` is an invertible sheaf on the
200	relative curve, representing a class in `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}(T)`.
201	
202	The assignment `D ↦ [O_{C ×_k T}(D)]` is functorial in `T` (pullback of an
203	ideal sheaf along `id_C ×_k g : C ×_k T' ⟶ C ×_k T` takes the ideal of `D`
204	to the ideal of `D_{T'}`, since `D` is flat over `T` and hence
205	`D_{T'} = D ×_T T'` is again a relative effective divisor by Kleiman §3
206	Lem. `lm:ctn`).
207	
208	Blueprint reference: `lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence` (Kleiman §3
209	Def. `dfn:Abel` + Thm. `th:repDiv`). -/
